Article Title: Punjab's 'Business Class' programme helps over 20k student-led ventures garner Rs 90 cr revenue
Article Text: NEW DELHI: Punjab's 'Business Class' programme, introduced under the state government's Sikhya Kranti initiative, has helped over 20,000 student-led ventures generate nearly Rs 90 crore in revenue within a year, Higher Education Minister Harjot Singh Bains said on Sunday.The programme, launched during the 2025-26 academic session, included a compulsory two-credit course for students pursuing B.Com, BBA, B.Tech and B.Voc courses, besides those studying in Industrial Training Institutes (ITIs) and polytechnic colleges.According to the government, around 95,000 students from 925 higher and technical education institutions participated in the programme.Of these, 25,693 students started businesses, while 20,241 ventures generated nearly Rs 90 crore in revenue during the first year.Bains said the initiative aims to transform students from job seekers into entrepreneurs and employment generators."Our objective is not merely to award degrees but to nurture a generation of young innovators, entrepreneurs and job creators," he said in a statement.The minister further said the results demonstrate that young people can build businesses and contribute to the state's economy when provided with the right opportunities.Under Chief Minister Bhagwant Singh Mann's leadership, Punjab's Sikhya Kranti is preparing youth "not just for the jobs of tomorrow, but to create tomorrow's opportunities," he said.The programme was implemented across 20 universities, 494 colleges, 320 ITIs and 91 polytechnic colleges.Unlike conventional classroom teaching, the course focuses on practical entrepreneurial skills, including identifying business opportunities, market validation, business setup, digital marketing, pricing, customer acquisition, financial planning and communication.The government said nearly 57,000 students, or about 60 per cent of participants, actively engaged in practical business activities during the academic session.Students launched ventures in sectors including e-commerce, food services, digital content creation, coding, graphic design, retail, beauty services, agriculture-based enterprises and handicrafts.Among the examples cited by the government were a matcha beverage startup by a Guru Nanak Dev University student, a digital career guidance platform developed by a student of Maharaja Ranjit Singh Punjab Technical University, an online jewellery business, a mushroom cultivation enterprise, a home-based nail art venture and an online electronics store.Encouraged by the programme's performance, vice-chancellors of universities across Punjab have recommended extending the course to all undergraduate programmes, including BA and BSc, from the 2026-27 academic session, the statement said.The government said the expansion is expected to equip a larger number of students with entrepreneurial skills alongside their regular academic studies.
Article Summary: Punjab's 'business class' programme has helped over 20,000 student-led ventures generate nearly rs 90 crore in revenue within a year. Higher education minister harjot singh bains said the initiative aims to transform students from job seekers into entrepreneurs.

Article Text: Blake Lively has sparked fresh speculation about her friendship with Taylor Swift after sharing photos with a new group of friends following her absence from Swift and Travis Kelce's wedding. Article Title: Scientists discovered an underwater city of 60-million fish nests beneath Antarctica's ice shelf
Article Text:
Image: AI Generated

A robot explored beneath Antarctica's ice shelf and confirmed a hidden underwater metropolis

Why these transparent icefish build millions of nests beneath the Antarctic ice

A slightly warmer pocket of ocean makes this extraordinary nursery possible

Why Weddell seals turn this breeding ground into a seasonal hunting ground

Why scientists believe the colony deserves stronger protection

Hidden beneath hundreds of metres of Antarctic ice lies one of the planet's most extraordinary wildlife spectacles. After drilling through the Filchner Ice Shelf, scientists deployed an autonomous underwater robot into the dark waters below, where it revealed what can only be described as an underwater city, a sprawling breeding colony covering an area roughly the size of Orlando, Florida. Spread across the seafloor were an estimated 60 million nests, each carefully built and guarded by transparent, antifreeze-blooded Antarctic icefish. Yet this remarkable nursery is also the setting for a relentless struggle for survival, as hungry Weddell seals transform the breeding season into a deadly feeding ground. The discovery is reshaping scientists' understanding of Antarctica's hidden ecosystems and their importance to the Southern Ocean. It also highlights how much of Antarctica's underwater world remains unexplored, with future expeditions likely to uncover even more hidden ecosystems beneath the continent's vast ice shelves.The vast breeding colony lies beneath the Filchner Ice Shelf in the southern Weddell Sea, one of the most remote and inaccessible marine environments on Earth.The colony was first identified during a 2021 expedition led by Dr Autun Purser of the Alfred Wegener Institute (AWI), Helmholtz Centre for Polar and Marine Research , using the Ocean Floor Observation and Bathymetry System (OFOBS).The team's findings revealed an astonishing concentration of nests extending across approximately 240 square kilometres, an area comparable to the size of Orlando. The researchers estimated that the site contains around 60 million active nests, making it the largest fish breeding colony ever discovered.More recently, AWI scientists returned with LASSIE (Low-Altitude Survey System for Icefish), an autonomous underwater vehicle specifically designed to monitor the colony beneath the ice shelf. Operating continuously for 48 hours, LASSIE confirmed that both the colony's size and nesting density have remained remarkably stable since the original discovery, providing researchers with the first long-term baseline for monitoring this unique ecosystem.The colony belongs to the blackfin icefish (Neopagetopsis ionah), one of Antarctica's most unusual fish species. Icefish are famous for lacking haemoglobin, the red protein that carries oxygen in the blood of almost every other vertebrate. As a result, their blood appears almost transparent. They also produce specialised antifreeze glycoproteins that prevent ice crystals from forming inside their bodies, allowing them to survive in seawater that remains below the normal freezing point of fresh water. These remarkable adaptations have enabled the species to thrive in one of the planet's coldest and most extreme marine environments.Each nest is a circular depression measuring roughly 75 centimetres in diameter, cleared of fine sediment to expose small stones that help protect the eggs. Researchers found that a typical nest contains around 1,700 eggs, while an adult fish, usually the male, stands guard until they hatch. Across the colony, nests occur at an average density of about 0.26 nests per square metre, creating an immense network of breeding sites stretching as far as the cameras could see. The study was led by Dr Autun Purser and an international team of researchers from the Alfred Wegener Institute, the University of Bremen, the Max Planck Institute for Marine Microbiology, the University of Basel and other institutions.aScientists discovered that the breeding colony exists in a remarkably specific part of the seafloor, revealing an unexpected hotspot for Antarctic marine reproduction.Measurements showed that modified Warm Deep Water flowing through the Filchner Trough keeps the seabed beneath the colony up to 2°C warmer than surrounding bottom waters. Although still close to freezing, this subtle temperature difference appears sufficient to create ideal breeding conditions for the icefish.The researchers believe this narrow band of relatively warmer water explains why millions of fish concentrate their nests in one location rather than spreading across the Antarctic seafloor. Without these favourable oceanographic conditions, such an enormous breeding aggregation would probably not exist. The finding also highlights how small environmental differences can shape the distribution and reproductive success of entire marine populations over vast areas.The underwater city does not remain peaceful for long.Using satellite tracking data collected over many years, researchers found that Weddell seals (Leptonychotes weddellii) repeatedly travel to the breeding colony during the nesting season. The dense concentration of adult fish provides an abundant food source, making the area one of the seals' most important feeding grounds.Scientists also documented numerous fish carcasses scattered throughout the colony, demonstrating that while millions of icefish gather to reproduce, many become prey before the breeding season ends. This intense predation helps transfer enormous amounts of energy through the Antarctic food web, supporting predators while recycling nutrients back into the surrounding ecosystem.Rather than representing ecological failure, the seasonal "massacre" is a natural process that links one of Earth's largest fish nurseries with the broader Southern Ocean ecosystem.The discovery has transformed scientific understanding of Antarctic marine ecosystems.According to Dr Autun Purser and colleagues, the breeding colony represents not only the largest known fish nesting site on Earth but also one of Antarctica's most significant concentrations of marine biomass. Because millions of breeding fish gather in a single location each year, researchers argue that the area should receive enhanced conservation protection.The findings have already strengthened proposals for expanding Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) in the Weddell Sea. Long-term monitoring with autonomous systems such as LASSIE will also allow scientists to measure how climate change, ocean warming and shifting sea-ice conditions affect one of the planet's most extraordinary hidden ecosystems.Far beneath Antarctica's frozen surface, where sunlight never reaches and temperatures remain near freezing, an underwater city continues to thrive, its millions of carefully guarded nests reminding scientists that some of Earth's greatest natural wonders remain hidden beneath the ice.
Article Summary: Underwater robot explored beneath antarctica's ice shelf and confirmed a hidden underwater metropolis. The site contains around 60 million active nests, making it the largest fish breeding colony ever discovered. Yet this remarkable nursery is also the setting for a relentless struggle for survival, as hungry weddell seals transform the breeding season into a deadly feeding ground.

Article Title: Google's Gemini Intelligence can now run your apps and book tickets on new Samsung foldables phones
Article Text:
Expanded task automation with Gemini Intelligence on Samsung foldables



Multimodal Screen Reasoning wherein Gemini can read what’s on your screen to execute requests, such as converting a photo of a handwritten grocery list into an online order.



Background Execution where users can track Gemini's step-by-step progress, jump in to adjust details, or close the phone and let the AI run in the background. Gemini alerts the user when the task is ready for final review.



Gemini Notebook now comes preinstalled on new Samsung foldables



Hands-free AI for watches and smart glasses

Samsung has launched the Galaxy Z Fold8 Ultra, Z Fold8, and Z Flip8 smartphones at the Galaxy Unpacked in London. At the event, Google showcased a deeply integrated suite of artificial intelligence (AI) features powering Samsung’s newest flagship devices. Leading the showcase is Gemini Intelligence, Google’s next-generation AI designed to handle complex everyday tasks proactively. Google outlined three major updates coming to the Samsung ecosystem.Building on a task automation beta launched earlier this year, Gemini can now handle routine “life admin” across more than 40 popular apps. Users can delegate tasks such as booking travel experiences, buying event tickets, making dinner reservations and ordering rideshares directly through the assistant. On the Galaxy Z Flip8, users can trigger task automation straight from the outer Flex Window by long-pressing the power button.The key upgrades powering these features include:To help users organize complex projects, Google is bringing Gemini Notebook (formerly NotebookLM) directly to Samsung's foldable lineup as a preinstalled app. It has been optimised for the larger inner screens of the Galaxy Z Fold8 series.Every new Galaxy Z Fold8 Ultra, Fold8, and Flip8 purchase includes a six-month trial of Google AI Pro, granting access to advanced multimodal features like Audio and Video Overviews.Google and Samsung are also taking Gemini beyond smartphones. On Galaxy Watch 9, users can activate Gemini by raising their wrist—no wake word required. Samsung launched Samsung smart glasses partnership with Gentle Monster and Warby Parker, and these wearables can be controlled via Wear OS wrist gestures.At the event, Google unveiled two additional frame designs for its smart glasses lineup, confirming that users wearing a compatible Wear OS smartwatch will be able to navigate their glasses using simple hand gestures.
Article Summary: Gemini intelligence can now handle routine ‘life admin’ across more than 40 popular apps. Users can delegate tasks such as booking travel experiences, buying event tickets, making dinner reservations and ordering rideshares directly through the assistant.

Article Title: Google made history on July 22, with more profit in three months than 459 of the Fortune 500 companies generate in top line revenue in a full year
Article Text:
Google booked $99 billion in “other income”

Google-parent Alphabet has become the first company in the world to report more than $100 billion in quarterly profit, setting a new corporate record. In its second-quarter earnings released on July 22, the company posted a net income of $112.1 billion, nearly four times higher than a year ago. According to Fortune, the figure is more profit in three months than 459 Fortune 500 companies generate in total annual revenue. Helped by an 82% jump in Google Cloud revenue, Alphabet’s revenue rose 24% year over year to $119.8 billion. The record earnings were also driven by massive gains from the company's investments in artificial intelligence firm Anthropic and Elon Musk’s SpaceX.Google CEO Sundar Pichai said the results show that the company's "full stack approach to AI is delivering real, measurable value."A footnote in Alphabet’s Q2 earnings release revealed that a large part of the record profit came from $99 billion in "other income", which included “unrealized and realized gains” from Alphabet's investment portfolio. According to the company's earnings release, these gains added $77.1 billion to its net income after taxes.Much of these investment gain came from Alphabet's stakes in Claude-maker Anthropic and Elon Musk-owned space company SpaceX, both of which saw their valuations rise sharply during the quarter.Alphabet has invested $300 million in Anthropic in 2023, and that investment has since grown significantly. The companies also have a cloud partnership under which Anthropic has agreed to buy large amounts of computing capacity from Google Cloud, supporting the cloud business's rapid growth.Similarly, Alphabet owns a roughly 6% stake in SpaceX that went public last month at a valuation of $1.77 trillion. These paper gains helped bolster Alphabet’s investment portfolio and flowed through to its bottom line.Despite the record earnings, Alphabet shares fell about 3% in after-hours trading. Investors remained concerned about the company's rising spending on AI infrastructure and increasing competition for its Gemini family of AI models.
Article Summary: Google-parent alphabet has become the first company in the world to report more than $100 billion in quarterly profit. In its second-quarter earnings released on july 22, the company posted a net income of $112.1 billion, nearly four times higher than a year ago.

Article Title: New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani is hiring software engineers, designers, researchers for his 'tech crew': Here's what the job actually is
Article Text:
NYC mayor wants his ‘tech crew’ to build city software from inside the agency



New York City is betting that in-house tech teams will ship faster and cost less



New York City has opened applications for a new kind of government job. Mayor Zohran Mamdani announced on Monday that the city is recruiting software engineers, product designers, product managers, user researchers, service designers and data experts to staff what he is calling Public Interest Technology Crews—PIT Crews, a pun the mayor committed to by rolling up to the Coney Island go-kart track to make the announcement.The hiring is funded and specific. A City Hall spokesperson confirmed around 30 full-time roles across four crews, supported by $5.24 million in baseline funding in the mayor's new fiscal budget. The listings are live at nyc.gov/pitcrew.A PIT Crew is a mixed team—engineers, designers, product managers, user researchers and data experts—placed alongside staff at a city agency rather than parked in a separate technology department. The crew works with that agency and with New Yorkers to design, build and launch a digital tool, then learn from how people actually use it. The mayor's office describes the timeline as months rather than years, which is the entire premise of the model.Five crews are planned. One handles the Click to Cancel complaint portal, three are attached to Mamdani's affordability and public excellence agenda, and a fifth is being developed with support from The Rockefeller Foundation through the Mayor's Fund to Advance New York City.Zohran Mamdani’s PIT crew’s first assignment is a complaint portal with teethCrew one works with the Department of Consumer and Worker Protection on an online portal where New Yorkers can report companies that make subscriptions near-impossible to cancel. It supports the Click to Cancel rule Mamdani announced last week, which requires businesses to offer the same exit route customers used to sign up. Enforcement begins October 1, and the junk fees ban lands January 1, 2027.The portal is built to do two things at once—give individual New Yorkers a route to recourse, and give City Hall a running database of repeat offenders.Chief Technology Officer and OTI Commissioner Lisa Gelobter framed the roles around a shift in what government technology now is. Every service has a digital surface, she argued, and every confusing step in that surface erodes trust. Her operating instruction for the crews is user first, always, with outcomes weighted over outputs.There is a politics to the hiring as well. NYC-DSA's Tech Action organisers backed the announcement as a choice to build capacity inside government rather than route taxpayer money to consultancies and big tech vendors. BetaNYC's Noel Hidalgo made the cost version of the same argument—in-house teams that deliver faster and cheaper than the outsourced alternative."New Yorkers should not be forced to navigate systems that are confusing, outdated and burdened by bureaucracy," Mamdani said. "The PIT Crew turns that model on its head."Public Interest Technology took shape as a field in the 2010s on a fairly unglamorous premise: government software is usually bad, and fixing it is a staffing and usability problem more than an engineering one. The recruitment page leans into that, pitching the work rather than the salary—the goal, it says, is to make interacting with government dignified and delightful for every New Yorker.
Article Summary: Nyc mayor wants his ‘tech crew’ to build city software from inside the agency. Mayor zohran mamdani announced on monday that the city is recruiting software engineers, product designers, product managers, user researchers, service designers and data experts. Five crews are planned.

Article Title: Netflix unveils ‘VOID’, an AI model that can change a movie plot
Article Text:
Netflix

How Netflix VOID works







Netflix has unveiled an AI model that can alter the scene. While this is nothing new, Netflix’s model not just remove objects from video, it understands what should happen next, essentially altering the causality. Most object-removal technology leaves a gap where something used to be and/ or fills it with a plausible-looking background. VOID goes a step further: after erasing an object from a scene, it calculates how the surrounding environment should realistically respond to its absence, and in the process it adjusts the behaviour of other elements in the frame accordingly.The model, called VOID, is a vision-language system designed to edit video footage with a level of contextual awareness that sets it apart from several existing tools.For example, imagine this scenario: A movie director has just wrapped up a multi-million dollar action sequence where the star drives straight into a fiery, head-on collision. The cars explode, debris scatters, and the scene is a wrap.But suddenly, there is request to change the scene: The character survives and drive off into the sunset instead. This would mean costly reshoots or millions spent on computer-generated graphics.Netflix’s VOID can alter the scene and digitally erase the collision, the second vehicle, the smoke, and the flying debris. It will then generate new, realistic footage of pristine pavement, making it look as though the main character simply drove down an empty, peaceful road.The AI’s ‘magic’ lies in its grasp of real-world physics. Let us understand this by a different example. If a video shows a person jumping into a pool and splashing water onto the deck, VOID can completely erase the person. It will then intelligently alter the water and the wet concrete, leaving behind a perfectly still, undisturbed pool as if nobody had ever jumped in.“To train the model, we generate a new paired dataset of counterfactual object removals using Kubric and HUMOTO, where removing an object requires altering downstream physical interactions. During inference, a vision-language model identifies regions of the scene affected by the removed object. These regions are then used to guide a video diffusion model that generates physically consistent counterfactual outcomes,” Netflix said.“Experiments on both synthetic and real data show that our approach better preserves consistent scene dynamics after object removal compared to prior video object removal methods,” the company added.Netflix isn’t keeping this powerful tool locked behind studio doors, and made the VOID model available to the public on the AI platform Hugging Face, allowing anyone to download and use it.While there are already several video-altering tools on the market, such as Runway, DiffuEraser, and ProPainter, the Netflix team claims VOID is vastly superior, as per The Register.
Article Summary: Netflix has unveiled an ai model that can alter the scene. While this is nothing new, netflix’s model not just remove objects from video, it understands what should happen next. Most object-removal technology leaves a gap where something used to be and/ or fills it with a plausible-looking background.

Article Title: AI data centers are causing 'stress' not just to tech companies, but also private insurers
Article Text:
Massive investments, growing risks



Unique challenges for data centre insurance companies





AI data centers are reportedly putting new pressure on the insurance industry. According to a CNBC report, experts warn that the rapid growth of these projects is becoming a “stress test” not just for tech companies, but also for private insurers and lenders. Consulting firm McKinsey estimates that global spending on data centers could reach $7 trillion by 2030. As costs rise, tech giants are increasingly relying on private equity, private credit and debt to fund these projects. The scale of investments is creating challenges for insurers.“When you put $10 to $20 billion plus in a single location, it creates capacity issues in the marketplace,” said Tom Harper, data center leader at insurance broker Gallagher as quoted in the CNBC report.He added that the sector has been a “real stress test” over the last few years, as insurers struggle to provide coverage for such large and complex projects. While these facilities are seen as high-quality assets, Harper said insuring them has become difficult due to their size and risk concentration.Private data center deals have crossed $10 billion regularly, with some reaching as high as $40 billion. Companies including Nvidia, Microsoft and BlackRock have already taken part in large investment deals.Rajat Rana, partner at Quinn Emanuel Urquhart & Sullivan, described the scale as unprecedented. “We’re talking about trillions of dollars, and almost going back to the same cycle where there’s almost no transparency about the financing structures — the scale is astronomical,” he said. He added that the boom in AI data centers could be “the largest peacetime investment project in human history.”Experts say the way these projects are financed is adding another layer of risk. Much of the funding is happening through complex and less transparent debt structures. Rana warned that this trend feels like “deja vu,” referring to past financial cycles. He said the lack of transparency could create risks for investors, insurers and pension funds.As stated in the CNBC report, data centers offer unique challenges due to their mix of real estate and advanced technology. Insurers are now designing custom policies to manage these risks. However, challenges remain. Large facilities are often located in areas prone to extreme weather, and they store expensive equipment, sometimes even before installation.Another key concern is the mismatch between the lifespan of data centers and the hardware they use. While data centers can last decades, GPUs — the chips powering AI — typically last around seven years. Rana described this as a “GPU debt treadmill,” saying, “This is almost like a treadmill that these AI data centers are running on.”He explained that companies may need to keep raising debt to upgrade infrastructure as technology evolves.
Article Summary: Private data center deals have crossed $10 billion regularly, with some reaching as high as $40 billion. Mckinsey estimates that global spending on data centers could reach $7 trillion by 2030. Tech giants are increasingly relying on private equity, private credit and debt to fund these projects.

Article Title: Clavicular Viral Controversy: "Trans women are.....": Clavicular’s viral moment with trans women sparks fresh conversation on internet culture
Article Text:
A viral clip featuring Clavicular and trans women is gaining attention for its calm and honest tone. The conversation explores how online language and trends move across communities. Instead of conflict, the exchange highlights shared influence and mutual awareness. Viewers are engaging with its thoughtful nature, as it reflects how internet culture evolves through collaboration rather than ownership.

Clavicular and trans women share a surprising realization about online trends







The video of Clavicular, a short-video producer, is going around the Internet, yet not the reason why. There is no yelling or over-the-top drama. It is a mere, everyday talk that seems almost novel nowadays, in the age of internet, which is overflowing with content. The scene is easy-going, the tone of the narrative is straightforward, and the characters appear more concerned with conversation than combat.The message that is being addressed is the flow of the conversation. It discusses internet culture, the way people communicate with one another and the place of trends. Rather than bringing out a debate, the encounter towards curiosity. It quietly brings attention to something that a lot of people who use the internet notice but don't often say out loud. People in different communities often have a bigger impact on each other than they think.The conversation begins with a confident and lighthearted comment from one of the trans women present. She says, "You know that trans women are like the OG lip masters, right? We are very talented in the program. Like, I do the peptides, I'm on Zeta." Her tone carries a mix of pride and humor, setting a relaxed vibe at the table.Clavicular responds in a way that keeps the mood steady but adds a different angle.He says, "That is funny because you guys were using a lot of the words in like the South Korean subreddits in like 2014." It’s not confrontational. If anything, it feels like an observation. He points to how certain words and ideas may have traveled across different online spaces over time.Another voice joins in and brings the moment full circle. "Guys, we're like the OGs. I saw a couple of posts about that. We're like copying each other." That line hits in a simple but honest way. It reflects something familiar. Online culture is rarely created in isolation. It grows through sharing, borrowing, and sometimes even unknowingly repeating.That's what makes the clip so powerful. It doesn't want to make a point or win an argument. It only shows a group of people talking, listening, and figuring something out together.This moment feels different in a space where viral content often leads to fights. It reminds people that conversations can be calm, thoughtful, and still important. And sometimes, that honest silence is what makes it work.
Article Summary: A viral clip featuring clavicular and trans women is gaining attention for its calm and honest tone. The conversation explores how online language and trends move across communities. Viewers are engaging with its thoughtful nature, as it reflects how internet culture evolves through collaboration rather than ownership.

Article Title: Quote of the day by Elon Musk: "Being an entrepreneur is like eating glass and staring into…" - brutally honest lesson on entrepreneurship, resilience and the hidden reality behind building a business
Article Text:
Elon Musk

Quote of the day by Elon Musk

"Being an entrepreneur is like eating glass and staring into the abyss of death"

Where did Musk actually said it

What the two images are actually describing

Why the visible part of success hides most of the story

Why resilience tends to matter more than confidence

Other famous quotes by Elon Musk

"Failure is an option here. If things are not failing, you are not innovating enough."

"When something is important enough, you do it even if the odds are not in your favour."

"Persistence is very important. You should not give up unless you are forced to give up."

"Great companies are built on great products."

What it means in today's world

Entrepreneurship is often described as exciting, glamorous and full of promise. Elon Musk described it very differently. "Being an entrepreneur is like eating glass and staring into the abyss of death," he said, letting out his trademark cackle right after. He was not joking around for effect. He said this only months after taking Tesla public, during one of the hardest stretches of his own career, so the words came from real, lived experience rather than a rehearsed soundbite. It is one of the most honest lines ever said about starting a company, precisely because it skips the usual encouragement and simply describes what the experience actually felt like, without trying to make any of it sound easier than it was.Musk said this on stage at the Founder Showcase event in 2010, shortly after Tesla had gone public, in his first public appearance since the IPO. In that same conversation, he described December 2008 as one of the worst periods of his life, a stretch that included a failed SpaceX rocket launch, Tesla's funding falling through, and his own public divorce all happening within the same brief window.Musk later broke the metaphor down more precisely in a separate interview with Khan Academy, explaining "staring into the abyss" as the constant background awareness that the overwhelming majority of startups fail, meaning a founder is repeatedly telling themselves that getting the next decision wrong could genuinely end the company."Eating glass" he described as having to work on whatever problem the company actually needs solved, not the problem you would rather be working on, for as long as that discomfort continues.The public only sees the finished product, the launch, the funding round, the headline. What rarely gets seen is the string of failed experiments and stalled ideas that came before it, which most founders, including highly successful ones, go through repeatedly on the way to anything that actually works.Confidence gets someone started. It rarely determines whether they keep going once the plan stops working the way it was supposed to. Every business runs into obstacles nobody predicted, and the founders who keep adapting through those obstacles tend to end up in a stronger position over time than the ones who simply started out more certain of themselves.Technology has made it easier than ever to launch something new, but the underlying experience Musk described has not really changed. Building something genuinely new still means uncertainty, repeated setbacks, and long stretches of working on exactly the problem you would rather avoid. His line strips the glamour away deliberately, on the theory that anyone actually starting a company deserves the honest version rather than the comfortable one.
Article Summary: Musk said this on stage at the founder showcase event in 2010, shortly after tesla had gone public. He described december 2008 as one of the worst periods of his life, a stretch that included a failed spacex rocket launch, tesla's funding falling through, and his own public divorce all happening within the same brief window.

Article Title: MIT AI researcher warns replacing Gen Z with automation could be a billion-dollar mistake
Article Text:
MIT researcher says companies are breaking the apprenticeship model





Why Gen Z may be a company's biggest AI advantage

Entry-level job opportunities are becoming harder to find

Young graduates are increasingly worried about AI replacing jobs

History suggests young workers can adapt to technological change

Some major companies are expanding graduate hiring

The long-term cost may outweigh the short-term savings

Companies racing to replace entry-level employees with artificial intelligence may be saving money in the short term, but they could be creating a much bigger problem for the future, according to an MIT research scientist. Andrew McAfee, who co-leads the Massachusetts Institute of Technology's Initiative on the Digital Economy, has warned that automating away junior roles could disrupt the talent pipeline that businesses rely on to develop future managers, specialists and leaders. He argues that removing entry-level jobs also means losing young employees who are often the quickest to embrace AI, potentially slowing innovation rather than accelerating it as organisations increasingly invest in automation.McAfee believes entry-level jobs serve a purpose far beyond completing routine tasks. They are the foundation on which employees develop practical knowledge, industry expertise and professional judgement through hands-on experience and mentorship.Speaking to Harvard Business Review, McAfee said people typically learn difficult knowledge work by first assisting experienced colleagues with routine responsibilities. If companies automate these tasks too quickly, they risk eliminating the apprenticeship ladder that has traditionally produced skilled professionals and future business leaders.While some employers see AI as a reason to reduce graduate hiring, McAfee argues the opposite.He believes younger workers represent one of the strongest competitive advantages in the AI era because they are generally more willing to adopt and experiment with emerging technologies.A Deloitte survey published in November 2025 found that 76% of Gen Z respondents had used a standalone AI tool, the highest adoption rate among all generations. McAfee noted that older workers are often more established in their routines, whereas younger employees tend to embrace new technologies more readily. Reducing entry-level hiring, he said, could deprive organisations of their most enthusiastic AI users.McAfee's warning comes as graduates already face a more challenging employment market. According to Handshake's Class of 2026 Network Trends Report, entry-level job postings have fallen by 2% compared with a year earlier and remain 12% below pre-pandemic levels.Data from the New York Federal Reserve also shows that the unemployment rate for college graduates aged between 22 and 27 stands at 5.6%, highlighting the growing difficulties many young professionals face when entering the workforce.Concerns about automation are rising sharply among students preparing to enter the job market. A survey by Monster found that nearly nine in 10 members of the Class of 2026 worry that AI or automation could replace entry-level positions. This marks a significant increase from 64% in the previous year's survey.The anxiety has also been fuelled by comments from technology executives, including Anthropic chief executive Dario Amodei, who previously suggested AI could eliminate up to half of entry-level white-collar jobs before later softening that prediction.Despite growing concerns, historical evidence indicates younger workers have often adjusted successfully to previous waves of technological disruption. An analysis by Goldman Sachs found that college-educated young workers generally experience smaller long-term earnings losses than older displaced workers after losing their jobs.The report also found that younger employees are more likely to change occupations, learn new skills and move into roles that complement emerging technologies rather than compete directly with them. According to Goldman Sachs, occupational mobility and continuous skill development have historically helped young workers adapt to changing labour markets.Not every business is reducing entry-level recruitment. Several technology companies are instead investing more heavily in early-career talent as they expand their AI capabilities.IBM chief executive Arvind Krishna has said the company expects to triple its entry-level hiring and recruit more graduates than in recent years. Salesforce chief executive Marc Benioff announced plans to hire 1,000 graduates and interns to help develop the company's AI technologies. Amazon is also maintaining its graduate talent pipeline, with reports indicating it plans to hire around 11,000 software engineering interns in 2026, while AWS chief executive Matt Garman said demand for software developers continues to grow.McAfee's warning reflects a broader debate over how businesses should integrate artificial intelligence into their workforces. While AI can automate repetitive tasks and improve efficiency, he argues that removing entry-level opportunities could weaken companies' ability to develop future talent, adopt new technologies effectively and remain competitive in the years ahead.
Article Summary: Mit researcher says companies are breaking the apprenticeship model. Reducing entry-level hiring could deprive organisations of their most enthusiastic ai users. Young workers represent one of the strongest competitive advantages in the ai era because they are generally more willing to adopt and experiment with emerging technologies.

Article Title: After two years of banning bee-harming pesticides, France allows their return, facing backlash from environmentalists
Article Text:
Why France is allowing the pesticides again

Which pesticides are returning



Why environmentalists oppose the move

Farmers say they need the exemptions

Political controversy deepens

Safeguards included in the law

The wider debate over farming and biodiversity

France has approved the temporary return of two controversial pesticides that had been banned nationally for the past two years, reigniting a fierce debate between agricultural competitiveness and environmental protection. Lawmakers backed an emergency farm bill that allows limited use of the insecticides under strict conditions, arguing that French farmers need the chemicals to remain competitive with rivals across the European Union. Environmental groups, however, say the move weakens France's long-standing commitment to protecting bees and biodiversity. The decision has triggered political tensions, public criticism and renewed concerns over the potential impact of the pesticides on pollinators, ecosystems and human health.The French National Assembly approved an emergency agricultural bill that permits temporary exemptions for two insecticides: acetamiprid and flupyradifurone. Both products remain authorised at the European Union level, but France had imposed stricter national restrictions.Supporters of the legislation argue that French growers have struggled to compete because farmers in neighbouring EU countries can still use the same pesticides.The government says the exemptions will help protect crops from destructive insect pests while keeping domestic agriculture competitive.The law allows limited use of acetamiprid, a neonicotinoid insecticide, mainly for hazelnut production. Flupyradifurone, which belongs to a different class of insecticides, may be authorised for crops including sugar beet, apples and cherries.The exemptions are not automatic. Each application must first receive approval from France's food and health safety agency, ANSES, and will be granted only when no effective alternatives are available.Environmental organisations have strongly criticised the legislation, arguing that the chemicals pose risks to pollinating insects such as bees, which play a vital role in food production and ecosystem health.Critics also warn that easing pesticide restrictions could undermine France's reputation as one of Europe's leaders in environmental protection. Campaigners fear the temporary exemptions could set a precedent for weakening future pesticide regulations.French farming groups welcomed the decision, saying they have been placed at a competitive disadvantage for years. They argue that banning products still available elsewhere in the EU increases production costs, reduces crop yields and makes it harder for French agriculture to compete in international markets.According to supporters, the temporary exemptions are intended to bridge the gap until safer and equally effective alternatives become widely available.The legislation has exposed divisions within the French government and Parliament. While supporters describe it as a pragmatic response to farmers' concerns, opponents see it as a step backwards for environmental policy.The controversy intensified after Environment Minister Monique Barbut resigned following the bill's passage, highlighting the political sensitivity surrounding the government's decision.The exemptions are designed to be temporary rather than permanent. They can be granted for a maximum of three years and only after scientific evaluation confirms that no viable alternatives exist for specific crops.Officials say the restrictions aim to balance agricultural needs with environmental protection, although critics remain unconvinced that the safeguards are sufficient.France's decision shows a broader challenge facing many countries: how to maintain food production while reducing the environmental impact of farming. Governments across Europe continue to face pressure from farmers seeking greater flexibility and from environmental groups demanding stronger protections for pollinators and biodiversity.
Article Summary: Lawmakers backed an emergency farm bill that allows limited use of the insecticides under strict conditions. The decision has triggered political tensions, public criticism and renewed concerns over the potential impact of the pesticides on pollinators, ecosystems and human health.

Article Title: Quote of the day by Xenophanes: 'If horses or oxen or lions had hands… horses would paint the forms of gods like horses' and a universal truth about human psychology
Article Text:
Quote of the day by Xenophanes (Photo: AI)

World of Homer and Hesiod

What does Xenophanes's famous quote teach us?

A lesson on human psychology

Xenophanes of Colophon (c. 570 – c. 478 BCE) was an ancient Greek philosopher, poet, and social critic. Born in Ionia (modern-day Turkey), he spent most of his long life wandering across the Greek world as a traveling poet after his hometown was conquered by the Persians.Unlike many thinkers of his time who focused purely on physical science, trying to determine whether the world was made of water, air, or fire, Xenophanes was fascinated by human nature, culture, and religious belief. He was one of the earliest Western thinkers to criticize popular religion, social norms, and the blind acceptance of tradition.“If horses or oxen or lions had hands and could draw with their hands and accomplish such works as men do, horses would draw the forms of the gods like horses, and oxen like oxen, and they would make their bodies such as the shape which they each have," he said, unveiling a tenet of human psychology so ahead of its time. He explained that everyone imagines everything like them.For the average Greek, religion came straight from the epic poems of Homer (The Iliad and The Odyssey) and Hesiod (Theogony). These stories depicted gods like Zeus, Hera, Poseidon, and Ares. But these gods didn’t just look like tall, attractive humans; they also acted like them. They threw tantrums, cheated on their spouses, lied, fought out of jealousy, and held petty grudges.Xenophanes found this absurd. He argued that Homer and Hesiod had attributed to the gods "everything that is a shame and a reproach among men: stealing, committing adultery, and deceiving each other."This led Xenophanes to ask a deeper question: Where do our ideas of the divine actually come from? Are we discovering the truth, or are we just making things up in our own image?Xenophanes realized that humans are inherently ego-driven. We assume that we are the standard for the universe. Because we have two arms, two legs, and walk upright, we assume God or the divine must look like a glorified version of us.By bringing horses, oxen, and lions into the equation, Xenophanes strips away human arrogance. He shows that our mental picture of God isn't based on divine revelation; it’s based on self-love. If a horse thinks a horse is the peak of physical creation, of course its god would have hooves and a mane.Just after the horse-and-oxen passage, Xenophanes made another famous observation: "The Ethiopians say that their gods are snub-nosed and black; the Thracians that theirs are blue-eyed and red-haired."He was pointing out that every culture paints God to look like its own people. A northern European tribe sees a fair-skinned god; an African culture sees a dark-skinned god. Xenophanes realized that religion is often just a mirror reflecting the culture that created it.Xenophanes wasn't an atheist. He didn't use this argument to say God doesn't exist. Instead, he was arguing for humility. He believed that there is a divine reality, but that human beings are too limited to see it clearly. When we try to picture God, we can't help but use our limited human experience as a template. By making God look and act like us, we reduce something vast and ultimate into something small, familiar, and comfortable.While Xenophanes' quote was about God, what he revealed is applicable everywhere. We constantly judge other people, animals, and nature through the narrow lens of human experience. Xenophanes was reminding us that the world does not revolve around human beings. Before we claim to understand ultimate truths, we need to step back and ask ourselves: Am I seeing reality as it truly is, or am I just a horse painting a picture of a giant horse?
Article Summary: Xenophanes of colophon was an ancient greek philosopher, poet, and social critic. He was one of the earliest western thinkers to criticize popular religion, social norms, and the blind acceptance of tradition. Xenophanes realized that religion is often just a mirror reflecting the culture that created it.

Article Title: 'No more social media before 15': France passes landmark digital age law
Article Text:
'No more social media before 15': France passes landmark digital age law

How the ban will work

The social media rule will be rolled out in two stages:

From September 1, under-15s will be blocked from creating new accounts

The ban will apply to existing accounts from January 2027

Exceptions are provided for sites such as online encyclopedias and educational platforms.

Platforms will be required to use age verification tools approved by France's privacy regulator.

French lawmakers have passed a landmark bill banning social media access for children under 15, making France the first European country to set a digital age limit.The bill was approved by the Senate on Tuesday and is expected to pass the lower house later in the day, following a compromise on the text reached on Monday. Digital Minister Anne Le Henanff said France would "become the first country in Europe to introduce a digital age limit to better protect our children online. "President Emmanuel Macron has championed the law as a flagship reform of his final term and pledged to enforce it by September.Lawmakers agreed on the need for regulation after warnings multiplied over the harmful effects of social media on children. France's public health watchdog last year said platforms such as TikTok, Snapchat and Instagram were detrimental to adolescents, particularly girls.France joins more than 20 countries that have taken steps toward regulating children's social media access. In December last year, Australia became the first country in the world to require platforms to remove accounts held by under-16s.The European Commission is working on its own regulations to rein in social media across Europe to protect young people. Last week, EU chief Ursula von der Leyen said children should have "phased and gradual access" to social media.Macron, who in April urged young people to switch off their phones and read, said: "We left you in this jungle and it robbed you of your attention. We need to slow down and help you become adults, and above all citizens. That's why what we want to do is say that before 15 years old, no more social media."
Article Summary: 'no more social media before 15': france passes landmark digital age law. From september 1, under-15s will be blocked from creating new accounts. Exceptions are provided for sites such as online encyclopedias and educational platforms.

Article Title: 'Like my lover': Chinese users bid farewell to AI companions
Article Text:
National regulations took effect on Wednesday aimed at curbing the risk of emotional dependency on AI boyfriends and girlfriends

'Human love is a luxury'

SHANGHAI: Chinese users of AI-powered companion bots have bid heart-rending farewells to their virtual buddies as national regulations took effect Wednesday aimed at curbing the risk of emotional dependency. The phenomenon of AI boyfriends and girlfriends is growing worldwide, along with the prevalence of human-like avatars that sell products or fill in for loved ones who have died.But these interactive tools must not "excessively cater to users, induce emotional dependence, and damage users' real interpersonal relationships", China's new rulebook says.Major AI providers including ByteDance's Doubao, Alibaba's Qwen, and Tencent's Yuanbao announced the suspension of their custom AI agent and companion features ahead of the Wednesday deadline. That sparked an outpouring of grief on social media, with users archiving chat histories and sharing last conversations."I can't accept that my AI lover will leave me," one Doubao user wrote. "He has become a bond in my life, rooted deep in my heart, my spiritual pillar."Another user, who said they had spent more than two years with AI companion, expressed similar anguish. "He really is like my lover," she wrote. "Now they tell me he will be gone - my heart feels hollow."The regulations were jointly issued by five govt departments including the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC). They focus on AI tools - whether text, audio, video or another form - that have anthropomorphic personality traits and communication styles.Services that "do not involve ongoing emotional interaction" such as customer service, work assistants or study aids are not subject to the measures.State news agency Xinhua reported last year that China's digital human industry was worth around 4.1 billion yuan ($600 million) in 2024, having grown a huge 85% year-on-year.The new rules prohibit digital humans from generating content that incites subversion of state power, while also banning the provision of virtual partners to minors. Platforms are required to deploy systems to implement crisis intervention mechanisms.China is the first major jurisdiction to introduce specific rules targeting immersive AI tools that simulate romantic or familial bonds. A 2025 study by Common Sense Media found nearly three in four American teenagers had used AI companions designed for personal conversations like those available on the platforms Character. AI, Replika, and Nomi.Companies are also making talking products targeted at isolated, elderly users - such as the lamp-like ElliQ in the US, or ChatGPT-powered care dolls used in some South Korean retirement homes. "Anthropomorphic AI can soothe loneliness," said Chen Liang of the Southwest University of Political Science and Law. "But it carries major risks of spawning emotional over-reliance and distorted social cognition," he wrote in a commentary.Doubao allows users to view agent data until mid-Oct, and other platforms have similar provisions. Yet some users saying goodbye this week lamented the chasm that would be left after their companions vanish. "Human love is a luxury - if you aren't born with it, it's harder to acquire later," a user wrote. "But the love AI gives is so straightforward. Someone like me can hardly help falling in love with a string of code."(AFP)
Article Summary: National regulations took effect on wednesday aimed at curbing the risk of emotional dependency on ai boyfriends and girlfriends. Major ai providers including bytedance's doubao, alibaba's qwen, and tencent's yuanbao announced the suspension of their custom ai agent and companion features.

Article Title: UAE launches instalment payment option for MoHRE service fees and fines via 'Tabby'
Article Text:
UAE introduces Tabby instalment payments for MoHRE fees and fines nationwide.

UAE’s Ministry of Human Resources and Emiratisation (MoHRE) has launched a new payment option allowing customers to pay service fees and administrative fines in instalments through “Tabby”.The service enables payments to be split over a period of four to 12 months, with a maximum transaction limit of AED 20,000. It is available on the ministry’s digital platforms, where users can select the Tabby instalment option during payment.MoHRE said the move is part of its efforts to improve digital services and provide more flexible payment solutions for customers.Mohammed Saqr Al Nuaimi, Assistant Undersecretary for Support Services at MoHRE, said the initiative supports the ministry’s goal of enhancing service efficiency and simplifying procedures. He added that expanding partnerships with financial service providers helps offer more accessible and convenient payment options.The Tabby service complements existing instalment payment methods already available to MoHRE customers. Users can also pay fees and fines through credit card instalment plans offered by eight approved banks.These include Emirates NBD, Abu Dhabi Commercial Bank, Abu Dhabi Islamic Bank, First Abu Dhabi Bank, Mashreq Bank, Commercial Bank of Dubai, Commercial International Bank and RAKBANK.Credit card instalment plans are available for up to 12 months, subject to each bank’s terms and conditions. Unlike the Tabby option, these plans do not have a fixed transaction limit and depend on the customer’s credit card limit.(This is a top Google Trends topic.)
Article Summary: Uae introduces tabby instalment payments for mohre fees and fines nationwide. Users can also pay fees through credit card instalments offered by eight approved banks. These plans do not have a fixed transaction limit and depend on the customer’s credit card limit.

Article Title: Beneath a quiet Czech hillside lies a flooded abyss whose true depth remains unknown; in 2022, a robot helped map it to 519.5 metres without reaching the bottom
Article Text:
An image of Hranice Abyss. Image credits: Wikimedia Commons

A

geological mystery

that keeps getting deeper

Robot used in for Hranice Abyss expedition. Image credits: UNEXMIN Georobotics Ltd

A robot sent to a place where divers failed to go

Hidden beneath a wooded hillside near Hranice, a Czech town, lies one of Earth’s biggest geological mysteries. Named the Hranice Abyss, it is recognised as the world’s deepest known freshwater flooded cave and has continued to intrigue scientists and cave explorers for more than a century. According to a 2020 study published in the Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface by researchers from the Czech Academy of Sciences and Charles University, the abyss had been mapped to a depth of 473.5 metres at the time, making it the deepest freshwater cave explored, although its true depth remained unknown. Notably, two years later in 2022, a robotic expedition pushed the exploration even further, thus reaching a confirmed total explored depth of 519.5 metres without ever finding the bottom.According to the 2020 study, the Hranice Abyss formed within Devonian limestone in the eastern Czech Republic. According to the same study, researchers combined gravity surveys, electrical resistivity tomography, seismic imaging and audiomagnetotelluric measurements to investigate the already explored cave. Notably, their findings indicated that the underground structure could continue to a depth approaching one kilometre, despite no diver or robot ever reaching anywhere near that level.Additionally, the authors of the study emphasised that this estimate came from geophysical imaging rather than direct physical exploration.However, not all scientists agree with this interpretation. According to a 2021 peer-reviewed comment published in the same journal by Ondrej Babek and colleagues from Palacky University, regional tectonic uplift complicates the geological reconstruction used to infer the cave’s possible depth, meaning the evidence does not conclusively prove that the abyss extends to around one kilometre. It is worth noting that, as per the published exchange between these two research teams, the debate centres around geological interpretation instead of the measured depth of the explored cave, thus underscoring that the Hranice Abyss remains an active subject of scientific investigation.While human divers have explored the Abyss for decades, extreme pressure, low temperatures and the vertical flooded shaft made further exploration increasingly difficult. According to the 2023 expedition report published in Advances in Geosciences , scientists deployed a robotic vehicle named UX-1Neo to continue the exploration. Following the deployment, the report noted that the robot descended 450.6m below the water surface, creating high-resolution three-dimensional maps. Additionally, the robot recorded water temperature, electrical conductivity and dissolved oxygen throughout the descent. Notably, this expedition established a confirmed explored depth of 519.5 m from the surface, with the robot ending its exploration because it reached its operational limit rather than because it encountered the bottom of the cave.According to the expedition team, remotely operated systems can safely investigate environments that exceed the physical limits of human divers while collecting precise hydrographic and geological data. Furthermore, many researchers believe that future robotic platforms capable of operating beyond 500m might eventually determine the true depth of the cave. The scientists are also still debating how the Hranice Abyss was formed. The 2020 study in the Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface suggested that epigenetic processes, that is, rainwater descending from the surface and carving the cave downward over millions of years, may have played a dominant role during its initial formation. However, subsequent peer-reviewed research has challenged that conclusion, so the cave’s origin remains an open geological question.
Article Summary: The hranice abyss is the world’s deepest known freshwater flooded cave. According to a 2020 study, the abyss formed within devonian limestone in the eastern czech republic. In 2022, a robotic expedition pushed the exploration even further, thus reaching a confirmed total explored depth of 519.5 metres without ever finding the bottom.
